summarization.a: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of the MoS₂/TiNTs heterostructure, including Ti foil anodization to form Ti nanotubes and subsequent ALD growth of MoS₂.
summarization.b: Top-view SEM image of pristine TiNTs, showing a uniform and well-aligned nanotubular morphology.
summarization.c: Top-view SEM image of TiNTs after 200 ALD MoS₂ cycles, demonstrating conformal coating of MoS₂ on the nanotube walls.
summarization.d: Raman spectra of TiNTs before and after ALD MoS₂ deposition, revealing the characteristic vibrational modes of MoS₂. The Raman spectra confirm successful MoS₂ coating on TiNTs, as evidenced by the appearance of characteristic MoS₂ vibrational modes, while the persistence of TiO₂ peaks indicates laser penetration through the coating to the substrate.
summarization.e: Cross-sectional TEM image of the MoS₂/TiNTs heterostructure, highlighting the core–shell structure.
summarization.f: High-resolution TEM image showing a continuous and uniform MoS₂ layer on the TiNT surface.
summarization.g: Selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirming the crystalline nature of the deposited MoS₂.
summarization.h: HAADF-STEM image with corresponding EDX elemental maps, confirming the homogeneous distribution of Mo and S over the TiNTs and successful formation of the MoS₂/TiNTs heterostructure.

data_extraction.d:
| Mode label | Raman shift (cm⁻¹) | Relative intensity | Note |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|-------|
| E_g       | ~145               | High               | Main TiO₂ anatase peak |
| B₁g       | ~395               | Medium             | TiO₂ lattice vibration  |
| A₁g       | ~515               | Medium             | TiO₂ mode  |
| E_g       | ~640               | Medium–low  | Higher-order TiO₂ peak |
| LA         | ~230                | Medium          | Longitudinal acoustic mode MoS₂ |
| E₂g¹      | ~380–390     | High               | In-plane MoS₂ vibration |
| A₁g       | ~405–410      | Very high     | Out-of-plane MoS₂ vibration |
| 2LA(M)   | ~450–460  | Medium        | Second-order MoS₂ mode |

d.question: Based on the provided Raman spectra, what is the primary spectroscopic evidence that confirms the successful deposition of MoS₂ onto the TiNT substrate?
d.answer: The appearance of two new, distinct peaks in the MoS₂/TiNTs spectrum—the E2g1  and A1gmodes characteristic of crystalline 2H-MoS₂—serves as the primary evidence for successful ALD deposition.

d.question: Compare the Raman spectrum of the bare TiNTs to that of the MoS₂/TiNTs composite. Aside from the new MoS₂ peaks, what happens to the original TiNT peaks (e.g., the Eg  mode) after coating?
d.answer: The intensity of the primary TiNT peak (the Eg  mode) is significantly attenuated in the MoS₂/TiNTs spectrum. This indicates that the conformal ALD-MoS₂ coating covers the nanotube surfaces, damping their Raman signal.

d.question: The new peaks at ~380 cm⁻¹ and ~405 cm⁻¹ correspond to the in-plane (E2𝑔1) and out-of-plane (A₁g ) vibrational modes of MoS₂. What does the frequency difference (~25 cm⁻¹) between these two peaks indicate about the deposited film?
d.answer: The frequency difference of ~25 cm⁻¹ is characteristic of few-layer or bulk MoS₂. This confirms the deposited film is crystalline 2H-MoS₂ and suggests it consists of more than a single monolayer.

d.question: A conformal, crystalline MoS₂ coating on TiNTs is sought for applications like catalysis or batteries. What does the clear presence of sharp (E2𝑔1) and  (A₁g ) peaks, without broadening or shifting, imply about the quality of the ALD process?
d.answer: The sharp, well-defined MoS₂ peaks imply the ALD process produced a coating with good crystalline order and minimal defects. This high material quality is essential for achieving optimal electrical conductivity and catalytic activity in functional applications.
